serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Burmese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component ingredients: rice noodles (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #20113), Centella asiatica leaves (FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data), dried shrimp, fish sauce, chickpea powder, sesame oil, and lime juice; proportions based on standard Burmese thoke recipe documentation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Myin Kha Yar Thoke exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy for rice noodles and chickpea flour, FAO/INFOODS SEA for Centella asiatica leaves and dried shrimp, and standard recipe proportions documented in Burmese culinary literature. Approximate recipe per 100 g of finished dish: rice noodles ~45 g cooked weight, myin kha yar leaf ~20 g, dried shrimp ~5 g, chickpea powder ~5 g, fish sauce ~5 g, sesame oil ~3 g, lime juice ~5 g, onion/garlic/chilli ~12 g.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Rice vermicelli GI ~53 (boiled).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Myin Kha Yar Thoke. The GI of 53 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source, boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low category), as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The addition of Centella asiatica leaves, dried shrimp, chickpea powder, fish sauce, and lime juice lowers the effective glycaemic response relative to plain noodles. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 21.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 34.2, rounded to 34.0, based on 300 g serving size and 21.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp (~480 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1440 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a single serving may contribute approximately 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (~34 per serving) driven by rice noodle carbohydrate content, partially offset by dietary fibre and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial blood glucose, given the moderate glycaemic load of approximately 34 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp; low saturated fat content is favourable, but high sodium poses cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content (~1440 mg per serving) and consider requesting reduced fish sauce to lower cardiovascular risk, while the low saturated fat profile (~0.5 g per 100 g) is a favourable attribute.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Myin Kha Yar Thoke (မြင်းခွာရွက်သုတ်) is a traditional Burmese noodle salad featuring myin kha yar leaves — the Burmese name for Centella asiatica, commonly known as pennywort or gotu kola — tossed with thin rice noodles (vermicelli), dried shrimp, roasted chickpea powder, fish sauce, lime juice, sesame oil, garlic, onion, and fresh or dried chilli. The dish belongs to the 'thoke' category of Burmese cuisine, which encompasses a wide variety of hand-tossed salads that are a defining feature of Myanmar's food culture. The noodles are boiled until tender, drained, and then hand-tossed with the remaining ingredients to ensure even coating. The resulting dish has a complex flavour profile: tangy from lime, umami-rich from fish sauce and dried shrimp, nutty from sesame oil and chickpea powder, and slightly bitter-herbaceous from the Centella asiatica leaves. The texture combines soft noodles with the slight crunch of onion and the chewiness of dried shrimp. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~138 kcal per 100 g), a reasonable protein contribution from dried shrimp and chickpea powder (~5.8 g per 100 g), and low saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) attributable to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at 53 (low category) based on the rice vermicelli base, with a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 34 per standard 300 g serving. Centella asiatica leaves contribute vitamin C, folate, and antioxidant phytochemicals. The dish is commonly served as a light meal or snack in Myanmar and at Burmese restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Myin Kha Yar Thoke exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for individual components. GI is estimated from the analogous rice vermicelli GI value. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients; no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for the component ingredients of this dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy data for the specific ingredient combination used in this estimation. 2. The food name 'မြင်းခွာရွက်သုတ်' translates literally as 'horseshoe crab leaf salad'; the leaf referred to is Centella asiatica (pennywort/gotu kola), called 'myin kha yar' in Burmese because its leaf shape resembles a horseshoe crab shell. 3. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Lor Mee', which appears to be a tagging error on the field survey platform; Myin Kha Yar Thoke is a Burmese thoke (salad-noodle) dish and is not related to Singaporean Lor Mee. 4. The food_group 'Grains and staples' from the input is retained as the primary carbohydrate source is rice noodles. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation; individual restaurant practices should be verified. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as the dish uses sesame oil and no hydrogenated fats are present in the standard recipe formulation.
